data.title: Workhorse Group Inc expected to post a loss of 42 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Workhorse Group Inc is expected to show a fall in quarterly revenue when it reports results on March 10 (estimated) for the period ending December 31 2024

  • The Loveland Ohio-based company is expected to report a 29.6% decrease in revenue to $3.1 million from $4.41 million a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 2 analysts, based on LSEG data.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Workhorse Group Inc is for a loss of 42 cents per share.

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is no "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Workhorse Group Inc is $1.00​, above​ its last closing price of $0.35. ​​​

data.title: Market Chatter: China Looks to Remove Price Caps on Local Government Purchases of Unsold Houses
data.description:
The Chinese government is looking to remove a price ceiling for local governments purchasing unsold apartments, Bloomberg news reported Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The proposal comes as Beijing is looking to dispose of millions of unsold homes and prop up the country's property sector, according to the report.

Local governments will no longer have a price cap equivalent to the cost of cheap homes in the same neighborhood, the report said.

City and provincial authorities would also have the freedom to offer competitive prices and help ease developers' financial burdens, according to Bloomberg.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Sri Lanka shares end lower as materials, information technology decline
data.description:
Sri Lankan shares fell on Friday, dragged by materials and information technology stocks.

* The CSE All-Share index settled 0.05% lower at 16,115.47.

* The index is down 2.20% for the week, marking its third straight week of falls.

* Sri Lanka and Japan signed agreements on Friday to restructure $2.5 billion worth of debt, completing two years of talks, that will assist the island nation in getting back on its feet after a severe financial crisis.

The agreements will allow the two countries to restart projects, including an expansion of Sri Lanka's main airport, which were suspended after the island nation announced in April 2022 that it was defaulting on its foreign debt.

* Paragon Ceylon Plc and York Arcade Holdings Plc were the top losers on the index, down 12.5% and 11%, respectively.

* Trading volume on the index fell to 67.6 million shares from 83.8 million shares in the previous session.

* The equity market's turnover fell to 2.18 billion Sri Lankan rupees ($7.38 million) from 2.36 billion rupees in the previous session, according to exchange data.

* Foreign investors were net sellers, offloading stocks worth 518.4 million rupees, while domestic investors were net buyers, purchasing shares worth 2.07 billion rupees, the data showed.

* For a report on global markets, click

($1 = 295.2500 Sri Lankan rupees)

data.title: Wells Fargo Adjusts Price Target on Adobe to $600 From $640, Keeps Overweight Rating
data.description: Adobe has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $570, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: India's IndusInd Bank CEO gets one-year extension, short of board's recommendation
data.description:
Indian private lender IndusInd Bank said on Friday it had received approval from the country's central bank to reappoint Sumant Kathpalia as its chief executive officer and managing director for a period of one year.

The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) approved Kathpalia's term to be extended from March 24 this year to March 23, 2026, IndusInd said.

The private lender's board in September had approved Kathpalia's reappointment as CEO for a three-year term.

Typically, the RBI gives a three-year extension period for the reappointment of CEOs.

However, there have been instances where the RBI gave one-year extensions to bank CEOs, with the most notable cases being those of former Kotak Mahindra Bank CEO Uday Kotak, former Federal Bank CEO Shyam Srinivasan and former RBL Bank CEO Vishwavir Ahuja.

Kathpalia, with 37 years of experience as a career banker, has been with IndusInd Bank for 17 years. He has also worked with global banks, including Citibank, Bank of America and ABN AMRO.

IndusInd is currently dealing with increased stress from its microfinance segment, where the gross non-performing assets ratio is deteriorating, resulting in the lender missing its third-quarter profit estimates.

Microfinance forms 9% of the bank's total loans.

data.title: ONGC Petro Additions Says Development Commissioner Grants Final Exit To Co From Dahej Special Economic Zone
data.description:
ONGC Petro Additions:

  • DEVELOPMENT COMMISSIONER GRANTS FINAL EXIT TO CO FROM DAHEJ SPECIAL ECONOMIC ZONE

  • CO SHALL OPERATE AS A DOMESTIC TARIFF AREA UNIT

data.title: Netel to Install Tele2's Broadband Network in Sweden
data.description:
Telecommunications operator Netel Holding signed a new two-year framework deal to install and maintain Tele2's (TEL2-A.ST, TEL2-B.ST) broadband network in Sweden.

The agreement marks a renewed collaboration between the companies, according to a Friday release. The deal took effect in January.

data.title: CAP-XX Inks Master Distributor Deal with US-based Waldom Electronics
data.description:
CAP-XX signed a master distributor agreement with US-based Waldom Electronics to extend its reach to high-volume global distributors, according to a Friday release.

The deal with the master distributor of electronic components, which only sells to distributors, will also let local and niche distributors buy CAP-XX products in smaller amounts, as well as streamline product availability and accelerate deliveries.

The initial inventory of the supercapacitor manufacturer's products will be set up in Europe, followed by the US and Asia.

data.title: ‘Merz spurt’ will see Europe CEOs rethink US pivot
data.description:
Donald Trump’s win made bosses from London to Rome even keener on a piece of the pro-growth action. Yet the US president’s tariffs and a fiscal boost from Germany’s Friedrich Merz are turning the tide. Surging stocks in Europe imply its CEOs may find more opportunities at home.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow @gfhay on X

CONTEXT NEWS

The STOXX Europe 600 Index rose 9% between December 31, 2024, and the morning of March 7, 2025. That compared with a 2% fall for the United States-focused S&P 500 Index.

Among the main European stock indexes, the German DAX performed the best, with a 15% rise over the same period.

data.title: Chile Inflation Rate Slows to 4.7% in February
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Chile decelerated to 4.7% in February 2025, from an over one-year high of 4.9% in January but remained above the central bank's 2% to 4% target range.

Slower price growth was observed for some CPI categories, including housing & utilities (11.6% vs 12.1% in January); restaurants & hotels (7.2% vs 7.8%); transportation (2.7% vs 3.1%) and food & non-alcoholic beverages (2.9% vs 3%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rose by 0.4% in February, slowing from a 1.1% increase in the prior month and matching market forecasts.

data.title: RBC Initiates GH Research at Outperform, Speculative Risk With $31 Price Target
data.description: GH Research has an average rating of buy and mean price target of $35.50, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: UIL Ltd. Novates Resimac Loan to Subsidiary
data.description:
UIL Ltd. novated the AU$11 million loan facility it signed with Australian non-bank lender Resimac Group in December 2024 to its subsidiary Pan Pacific Petroleum.

The British investment company also agreed to guarantee the repayment obligations of the subsidiary, according to a Friday release. The facility's repayment date was extended to May 31.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

President Trump partly pulled back his tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports, but investors are unnerved, and some goods are still exposed. As Elon Musk becomes more closely tied to Trump's politics, his electric-car company is losing customers. And Walgreens' new owner is embarking on a salvage project out of the public eye.

Today's Headlines

Trump's embrace of Russia has Germany thinking about alternatives to the U.S. nuclear umbrella.

Blackstone closed on an $8 billion commercial real-estate debt fund, matching its record for an investment vehicle that is helping fuel the property-market rebound.

Syrian government forces clashed with partisans of the deposed Assad regime, in a serious test for the former rebel group trying to hold the country together.

Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse is emerging as one of the biggest winners from Trump's crypto reserve plan.

China's top diplomat chided the Trump administration and said efforts to contain the country's rise were destined to fail, presenting Beijing as a force for stability.

Live From The Markets

Property and casualty insurers, despite inflation and recent disasters, are reporting strong results, Telis Demos writes.

Read It Here First

Tesla's fortunes are falling as Musk rises in Trump's world.

Elon Musk's pledge to reduce the world's reliance on fossil fuels and his push to broaden the appeal of electric cars attracted legions of buyers who shared his grand vision of techno-environmentalism. Now that Musk has allied himself with Trump and plunged into the deep end of national politics, many Tesla owners and would-be buyers are asking themselves what kind of statement it makes to get behind the wheel of a Tesla these days. Such doubts have begun showing up in worrisome numbers for the company, Becky Peterson and Sean McLain report.

Walgreens went from retail juggernaut to private-equity salvage project.

Walgreens — which became a ubiquitous seller of everything from diabetes injections to nail files — fell after it neglected to keep up with customer preference to buy online and failed to navigate the fierce competition and intense cost pressures of healthcare. It could shrink more after its sale to private-equity firm Sycamore. Joseph Walker explores the storied pharmacy chain's growth, even during the Great Depression, and the missteps that led to its decline.

Expert Take

Q: What goods are still getting hit with 25% tariffs?

Trump on Thursday suspended for one month the 25% tariffs he placed on Mexican and Canadian products earlier this week. But there's a catch: The deal only applies to goods that had been traded duty-free under a 2020 North American trade agreement. Chao Deng explains what products could still be subject to new tariffs.

A: Generally speaking, under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement, products enter the U.S. duty-free if businesses can show they comply with certain rules regarding the origins of those products' components. Because USMCA rules are so complicated, businesses have sometimes chosen to pay a tariff on a given product instead of expending time and money to figure out if it is USMCA compliant, according to trade experts.

The analytics firm Trade Partnership Worldwide estimated that in 2024, about 40% of U.S. imports from both Canada and Mexico fell outside USMCA but still passed through duty-free. From Mexico, such products included computers, medical equipment, phones and beer. From Canada, such products included petroleum gases, aluminum, airplanes and turbojet engines.

Only about 10% of total Mexican exports to the U.S. and about 22% of Canadian exports faced tariffs in 2024. For Mexico, this included auto parts and passenger vehicles that likely couldn't meet USMCA rules of origin. It also included low-tariff oil products in which suppliers opted to pay the duty rather than deal with the paperwork and compliance costs required to make a claim under USMCA. Canada's share was nearly all oil with very low tariff rates.

See The Story

U.S.-bound migrants stuck in Mexico face grim realities.

Migrants from Latin America and as far away as Afghanistan and China once flooded into Tapachula, a city near Mexico's southern border with Guatemala, and waited for their turn to cross the U.S. border. Now that the Trump administration has all but ended asylum as a pathway into the U.S., the migrants who fill Tapachula's shelters and churches are racing to find another way to America. Meanwhile, they're living with few options for jobs, housing or services.

Happening Today

The Labor Department releases its February jobs report at 8:30 a.m. ET.

Trump is expected to host and speak at the first-ever White House Crypto Summit.

Number Of The Day:

$300 billion

Russia's frozen overseas assets, which European countries have been divided over using since they put a halt on the funds days after Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine. But now that the U.S. is threatening to withdraw support for Ukraine, pressure is mounting on European countries to formally seize the funds and use them to help Kyiv.

And Finally...

Can James Bond be licensed to thrill again?

Now that Amazon's MGM Studios has taken over creative control of the Bond movie franchise, it has an opportunity to breathe new life into a stale brand and to remind audiences why they love spy stories in the first place.
